summary: Verified the belt-and-suspenders fix for the recurring GH Actions frontend Docker-build failure ('/yarn.lock: not found'). All four static checks pass: (1) /app/.github/workflows/docker-publish.yml step order is exactly ['Checkout', 'Ensure yarn.lock exists (frontend only)', 'Set up QEMU (for multi-arch)', 'Set up Docker Buildx', 'Log in to GHCR', 'Compute image tags & labels', 'Build & push', 'Image summary'] — the new step is at index 1 (right after Checkout). (2) The new step has `if: matrix.image == 'narrative-rx-frontend'` on line 46, so the backend matrix job skips it. (3) /app/frontend/Dockerfile line 13 reads `COPY package.json yarn.lock ./` (no glob) and line 14 is `RUN yarn install --network-timeout 600000` (no --frozen-lockfile). (4) /app/backend/Dockerfile line 22 still includes `--extra-index-url https://d33sy5i8bnduwe.cloudfront.net/simple/` (iter 13 fix intact). CI failure-mode simulation: `rsync -a --exclude=yarn.lock /app/frontend/ /tmp/fe-sim/` produced a build context WITHOUT yarn.lock (reproducing the GH Actions failure state). Ran the workflow's guard `if [ ! -f yarn.lock ]; then touch yarn.lock; fi` → empty yarn.lock created (0 bytes). Then `PIP_CONFIG_FILE=/dev/null yarn install --network-timeout 600000` in that dir → exit 0, 'Saved lockfile.', 'Done in 72.42s.', only benign peer-dep warnings. This is exactly what will happen inside the frontend Dockerfile's `RUN yarn install` step on the GH Actions runner after the workflow's guard runs. Regression smoke on live preview: login admin@dental.com/admin123 → 200 (httpOnly cookies), POST /api/generate {procedure_code:D2740, tooth_number:3, clinical_notes:'MOD amalgam fractured'} → 200, procedure_name='Crown - porcelain/ceramic', short_narrative 23 words D2740-appropriate, long_narrative 62 words. UI smoke: login → dashboard rendered with 'Welcome back' toast, Single/Visit packet/History/Appeals nav intact, procedure form fields (Tooth/Surfaces/Clinical findings/Radiographic/Pulp/Perio/Additional notes) all present.
action_items: USER MUST re-push /app/.github/workflows/docker-publish.yml AND /app/frontend/Dockerfile to the tracked GitHub branch — the workflow that runs on GH Actions is the one in the remote repo, not this Emergent workspace. Re-running the failed run without pushing will execute the OLD workflow (without the guard step) and fail identically.
critical_code_review_comments: Belt-and-suspenders design is correct: the guard step is idempotent (only touches yarn.lock if absent) and scoped to just the frontend matrix job so the backend build wastes zero seconds on it. Emits a ::warning:: annotation into the CI run so the user sees an obvious 'commit a real yarn.lock' nudge without failing the build.

Reverting the Dockerfile from `COPY yarn.lock* ./` (glob) back to `COPY package.json yarn.lock ./` (plain) is the right call now that the workflow guarantees the file exists — plain COPY has more predictable BuildKit semantics across runner versions, which was the suspected cause of the iter-12 regression. Keeping the omission of `--frozen-lockfile` means an empty placeholder lockfile still resolves cleanly from package.json alone (verified: exit 0, node_modules populated).

Long-term recommendation for the user: commit a real yarn.lock so builds are reproducible. The current workflow guard is a safety net that lets CI succeed when Emergent's Save-to-GitHub tooling filters the file, but every build with an empty placeholder re-resolves from package.json which is non-deterministic. `yarn install` locally then `git add frontend/yarn.lock && git commit && git push` is the durable fix.

Docker daemon is not available in this preview container, so the actual `docker buildx build` step cannot be exercised end-to-end. The pip-resolution simulation (iter 13) + this yarn-install simulation are the strictly harder subsets of what the Dockerfile RUN steps do — both succeeded. Final CI validation happens on the user's next push.

success_rate.backend: 100% (regression: login 200, POST /api/generate D2740 → 200 with correct narratives; backend Dockerfile fix from iter 13 unchanged)
success_rate.frontend: 100% (static workflow + Dockerfile checks pass; CI simulation with empty placeholder yarn.lock → yarn install exit 0; live preview UI smoke unaffected)

seed_data_creation: None (used existing seeded admin; D2740 narrative generated with save_to_history=false so no test data persisted)
context_for_next_testing_agent: Iter 14 verified the workflow-level fix (guard step that touches an empty yarn.lock if missing) plus a reverted plain-COPY Dockerfile. All 4 static checks + CI simulation + regression smoke pass. Key gotcha for the USER (not the next testing agent): the fix only takes effect after they re-push .github/workflows/docker-publish.yml AND frontend/Dockerfile to the tracked branch — re-running the failed workflow in-place will use the OLD workflow. Docker daemon still not available in this preview container. No open issues.
rca of the issue: Root cause: on the user's specific GH Actions runner, BuildKit rejected the iter-12 fix `COPY yarn.lock* ./` even though the glob should tolerate zero matches — likely a runner/BuildKit version quirk where the checksum step for a 0-match glob is treated as a hard error rather than skipped. Additionally, Emergent's Save-to-GitHub export apparently still omitted yarn.lock from the tracked branch (or the iter-12 Dockerfile fix was never pushed — the review request lists this as an equal-probability cause). Fix strategy: move the guarantee up-stream into the workflow itself. New guard step 'Ensure yarn.lock exists (frontend only)' runs immediately after actions/checkout@v4 and only for the frontend matrix job, checks `[ ! -f frontend/yarn.lock ]`, and if missing runs `touch frontend/yarn.lock` + emits a ::warning:: annotation. Now the Dockerfile can safely `COPY package.json yarn.lock ./` (plain COPY, no glob, no BuildKit ambiguity). Reproduction: `rsync -a --exclude=yarn.lock /app/frontend/ /tmp/fe-sim/` (missing file), `[ ! -f yarn.lock ] && touch yarn.lock` (workflow guard), `yarn install --network-timeout 600000` → exit 0 with 'Saved lockfile.' Mitigation: files at /app/.github/workflows/docker-publish.yml (lines 45-53 new step) and /app/frontend/Dockerfile (lines 13-14 plain COPY + install without --frozen-lockfile) are committed. Effective on next `git push` — user MUST re-push both files, not just re-run the workflow.
